sous_titres.text: Is that right? This is your husband's home.
So, could you tell us the events of the evening
the way you remember them, leading up to the shooting?
No, my memory's a bit fuzzy.
It was a party after all.
Certainly, I can do my best.
The first thing I remember seeing is
a bloody mess of a person dragged into my foyer.
Well, there was a lot of yelling.
Eileen Lovborg is on the phone.
She wants to talk to you.
I thought you never wanted to speak to me again.
I heard you're having a party tonight.
Yes, introducing Mr. and Mrs. George Tesman to society.
It'll be just like the old days.
{\an8}Please. I told you this morning, John,
{\an8}I want seven more lemons, all right?
{\an8}Ah, finally, there's a lady who knows what she's doing.
{\an8}Still, you can lay into it a bit more.
{\an8}And after it's rolled, you get into the potatoes. All right.
{\an8}Quick as a cat, now. Quick as a cat.
You're a bit early for a party, aren't you?
Buying you this house, Mrs. Tesman.
Don't point that thing at me.
Are you out of your bloody mind?
Oh, no. I didn't hit you, did I?
Does your husband know you're up there?
Judge Brack gave us the loan despite your antics.
The professorship and the endowment is how I pay him back,
so talk to Professor Greenwood tonight, would you?
I need him on my good side.
And he likes people like you.
He needs to have fun tonight, or I'll never hear the end of it.
I have friends, too, Hedda. Don't be rude.
Uh, it's your friends. Your friends are the ones...
Oh, trust me, George, they're well-behaved and well-bred,
and Greenwood will love them and you by extension.
'Cause nothing can go wrong tonight, Hedda.
Oh, and keep those guns out of sight.
Do the clasp at the top.
You know, I was... rather surprised that she'd been invited.
This is all happening because you wanted it.
Lot of money and effort was expended for you.
You will dance with me, won't you?
♪ If it smokes a great big cigar ♪
♪ and it hangs around at a bar...
He's business. He's functional. Know the difference.
Mr. and Mrs. Dunbar, I'm so glad you could make it after all.
Oh, good to see you. Thank you for coming.
Judge Brack, would you mind showing them in?
-Uh, this is my wife Tabitha. -How do you do?
She's duskier than I thought she would be.
What do you take me for?
Can't be one of George's friends, surely.
Oh, those are definitely Mrs. Tesman's friends.
I saw... I saw you talking
Um, how far back do you go with him?
I mean, it must be a good ten years now.
He may not recall me immediately, but, uh,
We weren't even sure we were at the right place
I'm sorry, Tabitha. What did you say?
Oh, just that we were so surprised
It's bigger than we thought it would be.
Judge Brack, have you met Professor Greenwood's wife, Tabitha?
Now, be a dear and get me one of these if you're topping up.
Oh, but I know you like your games.
Did I scare you off with my gun earlier?
You haven't come around to say hello.
A Mrs. Ellison is at the door.
So, tell me, what type of paintings do you do?
Oh, no. Did you walk here?
Mrs. Tesman, I am so sorry to interrupt.
I had no idea you were in town, Mrs. Ellison.
Of course, I would have invited you.
I just wanted to talk to you about, um...
-Well, I... -Let's get you out of these clothes.
-You have my undivided attention. -I just need to talk to her.
-How have you been since, uh... -Secondary school.
How are things going at home?
Please stop. Don't call me that.
Well, let's get you out of these clothes... Thea.
You can talk to me, school friend.
You used to pull my hair.
Every time you passed me on the stairs.
Once, you told me that you would burn it all off.
Amazing I never got around to it.
"I think this gown might do it."
Will you be gone from home long?
What will you do on your own?
I cowrote the last book with Eileen.
The one that sold out two weeks ago.
Well, my name isn't on it.
But we finished the first draft of the next one, and...
My name's on it, right underneath hers.
Uh, Thea Clifton. I don't want my husband's name on it.
No, just your father's. Raise your arms.
She's a bit rough around the edges.
Last I heard, she'd been arrested for being drunk and disorderly.
She's different now. She's softer, more careful.
I got her to stop drinking.
She understands how bad it is for her now.
-Uh, I don't think so. -Oh, no, don't be shy.
Seems you have rehabilitated our little love bug.
And you are here to make sure that she stays out of trouble, is that it?
And you left your husband a bit sooner than you planned?
We need another chair and setting.
Remind me, you do, uh, translate
I would love to, um, pick your brain.
Y-Y-You wouldn't believe the things I found in these libraries: old documents,
notes, papers that no one even knew existed.
You certainly made the most of your honeymoon.
So, you were doing research on your honeymoon?
Nothing else a bit more lively?
Well, we ate at a lot of very excellent restaurants.
Ah. Yes. Well, she does love eating out.
You must be one of Mrs. Tesman's friends.
When I'm in her good graces.
James... is that a first or a last name?
Uh, last. Uh, Professor John Henry James.
There's this woman that Eileen used to be...
And she says that she tried to shoot her
People don't do things like that here.
If she's here when Eileen comes,
then I'm worried that things might get out of hand.
Eileen will be here soon enough, and you can fear for her safety then.
I just don't want her to lose this opportunity.
Oh, she won't admit it, but she really needs this job.
Not just for her well-being but for her debts.
And she's bringing the new manuscript to show Greenwood.
Hopefully, it seals the deal, but I...
Let's not talk business at the table.
I'm not worried at all about it.
I'll get this position and, uh, begin payments...
Eileen Lovborg is applying for your position
at the university, George, endowment and all.
I'm in competition with that woman?
She cozied up to Professor Greenwood.
What does she get up to?
Oh, I'm sure you've heard the rumors.
I've heard she's reformed, changed her ways.
While she was at the Ellisons'.
We got married, we borrowed money, we went into debt,
because the position was virtually mine.
That was before the reappearance of Eileen Lovborg.
Don't be afraid of a little competition, Tesman.
I can't wait to see how it turns out.
I already told him there's no big rush.
Although I do wish we'd have been a bit more economical.
-Lovely house, George. -I'm glad you like it.
Some of your wife's friends are interesting characters.
Don't say the key to your heart.
I found it in the attic.
So it was General Gabler's gun you were shooting
Sure I can't buy them from you?
It's the only thing he left me.
I know some bastard children that have fared far worse than you.
Do you know what my father used to call me?
So he gave me his prettiest things.
You have always been a pretty little thing.
I thought you were having a marvelous time.
What do you know of Eileen Lovborg's reappearance?
-What's she like? -Well, it's as you said.
She's on the straight and narrow.
She still has some seedy friends, but they're all good fun in moderation.
She has a hold over you.
No one has a hold over me.
This house is between you and Tesman.
This house was only ever for you.
You, too, think that this house is my life's great passion.
Tesman used to walk me home last summer after those dinner parties,
and we passed by here one night, and there was a lull in the conversation.
I bought this house on a lull?
He was a nervous wreck trying to think of something to say to me.
I suppose I felt bad for him.
On a whim, I just said I wanted the house.
The next thing I knew, I was married to him.
And my whimsicality had its consequences.
As it often does, Hedda, for us all.
I just do things all of a sudden on a whim.
Nothing that would make George the wrong choice.
You are still General Gabler's daughter, and that woman is...
You have no power over me.
Well, if I've unwittingly resigned myself to a life of poverty,
I might as well go down in style.
I felt like, um... like I couldn't breathe.
Uh, it's happened to me before, but, um...
and I will retrieve you when, uh, Dr. Lovborg comes.

What is the matter with you?
I thought this was going to be a rager. You seemed absolutely ready for carnage

It seems some of my guests are getting restless.

Georgie! Sorry, you don't mind if I borrow him, do you?
It has been a long, long time.
Thank you for having me, Tesman.
Uh, please come in. Uh, have a drink.
I've brought a couple of friends, if that's all right.
Down, boy. This is her husband, Dr. George Tesman.
You said that they would behave.
I hear that your new book has been selling quite well.
Yes, I've just got a copy of it myself.
I haven't had a chance to read it yet, but, um...
Well, there's no need to be humble, Dr. Lovborg.
I'm not. There's nothing much to it, really.
so I put nothing remotely controversial in the book.
No one's scandalized by people fucking on millennia-old clay pots.
Something of a sequel to the first.
Well, what's there left to talk about?
What could we know about the future of th-that or anything?
We can point to some trends.
Well, I would never have thought to write anything like that.
Lovely to see you looking so well.
-Drink? -Very, very tempting, but no, thank you.
Some water or a soft drink for me, please.
It's the only complete copy I have,
and you're not getting your hands on it.
How could you throw yourself away?
He has a good job, or will.
When will you understand that anything you need in life,
What will you do when he gets bored?
If he leaves you, once he sees through you?
I think I'll be all right.
He goes on and on about how he wants to take care of me,
His ambition is deeper than his pockets.
He promised my father he'd look after me.
You're happy now, all of a sudden.
And the view from Valle d'Ampezzo was absolutely breathtaking.
I hear you've been cured of your vices.
You're never cured of your vices.
Going on three months, three days.
Your beliefs are no business of mine.
The woman I knew couldn't stop when she started.
Which is why I never start.
you were so drunk and high, if I remember correctly.
Which is why I never start.
Has she spoken to you since?
Will she let you around the children yet?
I'm just annoyed with myself that I ever confided in you.
You think I had a power over you?
The kind intelligent women wield when they're catastrophically bored.
I'm tired of running around this ridiculous hedge.
I'm back in town less than a day,
and I'm already wrapped up in your frivolous life.
scrambling about like a roach, trying to shape
I left my mother's house when I was 17 years old.
When she found your lesbian sex book, yes.
I've done my own research since.
Where no one can see or suspect, keeping your reputation clean.
Hedda, you could be so much more.
Tell me, how many women are at the university teaching?
And they're both white, I presume.
You're upset I couldn't choose you.
I saw her little bag near the door.
Do you know the roach can live without its head for a week?
You're quite the duo, you and Thea.
She's just been bullied into stupidity.
Leaving her husband for a promise and a credit is quite something.
Why didn't you shoot me back then?
Because I knew you really wanted me to.
That wasn't my greatest act of cowardice.
I called at your house, but they said you had gone.
You don't seem glad for the company.
I'm just thinking about when everyone leaves, how...
it's a big house, isn't it?
If I can't even entertain as I expected...
I wonder whether I couldn't get Tesman interested in a political career.
Political life is not for him.
He is aggressively unsuited to it.
Perhaps, but couldn't I ease him in that direction, make him take it up?
-Why put him in a position to fail? -I've told you.
Do you think it absolutely impossible for him to become, say, a cabinet minister?
Uh, for him to become a cabinet minister, he'd need to have money, a lot of money.
I'm as surprised as you by the new Lovborg.
And they like that these days... in moderation.
I'm sure you'll have some greater responsibility,
You won't ever see anything like that happen.
The only choice, really, for someone like you.
Just play the part and stay out of trouble.
Make sure Professor Greenwood never has an empty glass.
-Oh, I'll have a Manhattan. -Very good, sir.
I've heard so much about you, Mrs. Tesman.
Hmm. Not enough to know not to call me that.
That before you were domesticated, you were like fire.
but you're far too sober for that kind of honesty.
I won't be a ruined woman.
And when they publish our book, there's nothing
anyone will be able to say against us.
Won't know their ass from their elbows.
Hedda, is it okay if I borrow your dress for the evening?
I'll have it cleaned and sent back by tomorrow.
Oh, you're not leaving, are you?
Oh, come on, Eileen. You're stronger than that.
-Let's go. -Oh, shame. I just came up to let you know
that Professor Greenwood wanted to talk to you about your book... the new one.
What were you doing up there?
Have you even spoken to Professor Greenwood yet?
The core thesis frames empathy as a disorder.
I-I feel I've come to a similar conclusion myself.
-Where have you gone with it? -Well, empathy...
Oh, Hedda is going to cause some trouble.
Just stay out of the path of destruction.
When are you going to talk to him?
Professor Greenwood, do you approve of Scotch?
Well, then you need another drink.
You've come to show him the book, so show him the book.
Oh, Professor, I'll-I'll help you get a drink.
He'll see how much you've changed. Everybody will.
There's nothing to be afraid of.
I should leave before something terrible happens, shouldn't I?
Best time to leave a party is after something terrible has happened
I wanted to be sure we had fun.
Couldn't just talk about tombs, books and the Bronze Age all night, could we?
Can't wait to read your book, Eileen.
I could get you an early copy.
If you wanted to write an early review in your journal.
I'll see if we can find some space.
Well, I ought to be going.
I think she's in the maze.
I shall be seeing you soon.
-Off so soon, Professor? -Thank you so much.
Yes, it's amazing. It's really happening.
I thought you were just cutting back.
-That's not what I said. -And if I say you have to?
-You wouldn't do as I say? -Not where that is concerned, no.
-Really? -It's all right for Thea but not for you.
You can write your books with them and teach with them at the university,
and you might even be able to get jobs alongside them,
but they'll never really respect you if they think you can't do it
when you asked for a soft drink like a soft woman.
Contempt for your extracurricular interests, yes,
but for your mind, your character?
He can think what he likes.
What did I tell you earlier?
This state of mortal panic. Has it come to this?
In mortal panic on my account?
She only asked that Tesman look after you at the university.
-Look after me? -Make sure you stay out of trouble.
You run here and you involve my colleague in my business?
From the academics to the gossiping ladies in the corner.
Call me Dr. Lovborg when we're around my colleagues.
I think I'll go and speak to your husband,
You must know who she is by now.
You don't want her in control.
Perhaps I should burn it off after all.
If you ruin this for her, she'll never forgive you.
Do you want to go for a swim?
But I don't have any trunks.
Dr. Lovborg, may I borrow you?
What are you so afraid of, Thea?
No, I'm fine here near the fire.
If you don't bend, you'll break.
I'm not a bore. It's cold.
Will you tell her to get the stick out of her ass and get in the lake, please?
Thea, get that stick out your ass and get
You'll let me know if this gets out of hand.
Certain liberties come with power, George.
Don't make a thing of it.
She'll be fine. Let her cool down.
She's going to make me pay for that in the morning.
You just do whatever you want, don't you?
Otherwise, what would be the point?
Who was that? Who was that man?
Oh, my God. Is that David?
Come on, come on. We can't miss this. Come on.
I'm gonna shoot whoever's been sleeping with my wife.
You're going to run out of bullets.
What sort of gun is that?
Mm, you are what you shoot.
That's what my father used to say.
Looks like a Webley to me.
Do you know, I would shoot him, too.
But... if you put this down now, all will be forgotten and forgiven.
No crime has been committed yet... yet.
First day back, and they're dead set on destroying the place.
This house as nice as yours?
No, but I think my folk have a bit more money.
If they have any money, they've got
This house is headed straight back to the bank.
Maybe this is their last hurrah before they shut half of it down
and let the tours come through.
All part of the deal, apparently.
They get a grant to do the renovations, they do the upkeep,
they get to keep it for very, very little.
Well, little for their lot, not for us.
Practically in ruins before they came along.
Don't make much sense to me,
but the mistress was desperate for it, apparently.
She can't even fill the servants' quarters.
These girls are all hired for the night.
I'm the only staff they've got, and they got me from his aunt.
Do you know, I've got a son just like him.
Never been happier, not like Tesman.
The more he gets, the more he wants.
And now he's gone and got married.
Just want to look at you.
Why did you make me drink, Hedda?
I wanted to talk to you.
...you and I, if I'd said yes...
I've tried not to think about you, Hedda.
I would've, could've been your partner in all of this.
Build something with you, could've been...
I used to think I could never be happy.
What I was owed for a vile life.
I've since learned that I'm not that way at all.
But you, you can never be happy.
I think you were right about that.
I would have been dead, by your hand or mine, if I'd stayed.
To make sure I didn't love you.
Thea left her life for me.
She showed me how to be brave.
I had to make sure I had all of myself to give.
You have it all worked out, then.
But first, finish what you started.
You've played with the girls. Now go and be with the boys.
We worked on the book together.
For women they learn from, occasionally steal from
and don't wish to credit but absolutely want to fuck.
-Is it in your book? -What else is in your book?
How did you come up with it?
I knew I had to write this book
after a quite elucidating conversation with a young man at a bar.
I went with a friend of mine.
You might have met her here... Diana.
just before closing, and we thought, "Oh, why not?
She likes a tableau, a bit of a show,
We go in, grab a seat at the bar,
I light a cigarette, Diana gets her whiskey,
and we have a nice chat with the men tending.
And there were two of them.
The forgotten bastard children of lords.
We have a bit of a flirt.
Obviously with me, it's not going anywhere,
and after a while, they started to bore us,
so we retired to a table.
...Diana is complaining about her dull husband Richard,
and I'm listening as much as one who's already decided
not to waste one's days trying to please a man can.
And then my weak-chinned bartender comes over,
slides into the booth next to me, and he says,
Of course not, you gang of perverts.
I told him I preferred my sexual encounters
to be mutual and in private.
Maybe all people have a thing that they like,
that is different, that is new, maybe.
And as some past hidden things have become normalized,
I wonder, why shouldn't hidden things in the now
So we'll all be smelling feet and having a toss in ten years' time.
Who knows what you get up to, Judge Brack?
I'm sure it's going to be far more scandalous.
So, what are some of these new things?
You'll have to buy the book.
Or come to one of my lectures at the university.
I promise I'll give all of your wives a copy of the book for free.
Do you resent fun, you miserable creature?
If you wish to leave, leave.
There's no use waiting on her like a handmaid.
All right. Then go in and tell her to put down her glass.
And if I do get it, there are many, many universities that would have you.
Never known her to miss the company of rowdy men.
Well, you don't know her very well, then, do you?
I have known Hedda Gabler since she was a child
parading around with her father on their prize horses.
They'd go galloping past my house every Sunday.
She'd be in that black riding outfit... you know the one...
-The feather. Mm-hmm. -With the feather in her hat.
she would lean over to me, and she'd say,
"Look at that bastard making a show of herself."
What else should we do with the evening, boys?
I'm fine. I'm fine. I'm fine.
If you get your kit off.
Read us something from your new book.
Or your old one. I'm not fussed.
You're making a fool of yourself.
and you'll just have Hedda Gabler running around
doing whatever and whomever she pleases.
Hedda Gabler doesn't love anyone but herself,
How can you restrain a woman like that?
She's a prize, to be sure, but she's not a horse.
Sometimes I feel I could just take a riding crop to her.
It's another thing from my book.
I don't think I've ever heard you curse before, Mr. Tesman.
Well, what a way to make an entrance, eh?
I don't know what you're talking about!
You need to stop it! That's enough!
-I am so sorry. -Are you all right?
But for God's sake, what are you doing?
Eileen, I have no idea what you're talking about.
Yes, of-of course I have, yes, and-and then sold it off
We're not leaving the house until it's found.
Uh, if we could all please help, um...
if we could all help look for Dr. Lovborg's manuscript.
-Oh, oh, oh, oh! -Okay, okay. Oh, yes, yes, yes.
-Oh, I felt that one, yep. -Okay. Okay.
Uh, let's just find the fucking manuscript.
Let's just find the bloody thing.
It's just all this bad feeling.
It's just... it's too much sometimes, you know?
What... wh-what are we looking for again?
Uh, ch-check under the seats. It could be...
The manuscript, it must have, uh...
No, no. No, no, no, no, no, no, no, don't.
Eileen, you've had far too much to drink.
-Eileen, it's gone. It's gone. -Oh, no. No!
I've been looking for you everywhere.
Don't look at me like that. I know what I'm doing.
I'm going to put an end to her reign of...
some weapon to use against that woman.
You should pretend you never met me.
It's at the bottom of the lake.
you're always going to be on this path.
Only as much as you love me.
Can that be enough for you?
You're asking for more than I can give.
More than anybody has the right to ask for.
Well, there's the old Eileen... miserable and deluded,
-right where you're happy. -You use me, and then you fuck off to...
You use me so you can feel like your life means something.
I'm going to find my book.
And it will be published with my name on the cover
And then none of this will even have mattered.
This is exactly what you wanted.
I wanted you to be true to yourself.
You needed to have strength in who you are.
I have none left for this life.
All of my colleagues have seen me make a fool of myself.
The one person who mattered to me is gone.
I can't ride out another disgrace, Hedda.
being whomever you choose has its limits.
Where do you find your power, Hedda?
everything I am, everything I have,
is because I am fucking brilliant.
Because I can... I can write,
and because I convinced those dick-swinging troglodytes
that they had to listen to me.
I would have been protected from their ridicule because...
And now I'm alone, and I am nothing.
Where will you go from here?
I'll make an end of it as quickly as possible.
I want you to have this.
You should have used it then.
We-we-we... we have to find the manuscript.
I just saw Eileen. She's a wreck.
Here, some of it can still be saved.
Are you out of your mind?
N-N-No. W-W-Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait.
You can save some of them.
If that's what you really want, George.
But if she finds it, she will get your job.
I'm just looking for the manuscript.
I think all hope is lost for that.

I wanted you out of that woman's shadow.
I couldn't bear the idea of you living that way.
No, no, you just wanted money.
-You wanted... -I wanted you to have what you wanted.
I wanted you to be happy.
Oh, I am looking at you, Hedda.
Hope you're in a better mood.
-for God's sake. -You're not even gonna apologize?
We need to call an ambulance.
I didn't mean it. I didn't mean it.
And did you see the gun at any point after that?
Uh, no, no, no one has seen it since.
Is that right? This is your husband's home.
So, could you tell us the events of the evening...
I think sections, because it's scattered and...
We're going to reconstruct the book.
We think we can have it ready in time for the next term.
If she dies, you'll have all the money.
If this isn't completed in time, Dr. Lovborg will have nothing.
If you don't focus on your own research, Tesman...
We will move in to a smaller house.
Um, now, these sections, I think...
Can I be of any use?
Are you worried about something, Mrs. Tesman?
It's all very worrisome, isn't it?
How was Eileen when you saw her last?
You found yourselves alone last night, didn't you?
In the midst of all the madness.
Have they asked you about the gun?
The one at the center of all this.
The one that Eileen shot herself with.
It's nowhere to be found, apparently.
Professor Greenwood thinks it's his 'cause he lost track of it last night.
I'm sure they'll want to speak to him soon.
They'll want to see you next.
Ah. And what will I say?
Two guns were fired last night.
I'll say I have the gun that shot Eileen Lovborg.
It was a good idea, Hedda.
I never thought she'd do it here.
I thought she'd find somewhere beautiful.
when they find out the gun belonged to the great General Gabler.
That his pretty little bastard gave it to a lesbian
who tried to kill someone in the house our little Hedda can't afford.
The thought hadn't crossed my mind.
